summary: This book review discusses a critical edition of David Hume's 'Essays, Moral, Political, and Literary'. It highlights Hume's deliberate stylistic shift from the 'abstruse' philosophy of his early 'Treatise' to a more accessible essay format, aiming to bridge the gap between academic learning and everyday public discourse without sacrificing philosophical precision.

principles.statement: Philosophy must strike a balance between the 'abstruse' and the 'easy and obvious'.
principles.rationale: Abstruse philosophy ensures exactness and precision but risks becoming dull and irrelevant. Conversely, easy philosophy engages common life and influences conduct, but risks becoming shallow if it lacks rigor.
principles.source_quote: An author is little to be valued, who tells us nothing but what we can learn from every coffee-house conversation

principles.statement: Learning must not be isolated from the public sphere.
principles.rationale: When philosophy is confined to academia, it creates a schism between the 'learned world' and 'common life', preventing it from positively influencing human sentiments and conduct.
principles.source_quote: shut up in the Colleges and Cells, and secluded from the World and Good Company

mental_models.name: Two Species of Philosophy
mental_models.description: A lens for evaluating intellectual work by dividing it into two categories: the 'abstruse' (abstract, precise, but often disconnected from reality) and the 'easy and obvious' (accessible, practical, and engaged with common life).
mental_models.example: Hume used this model to evaluate the failure of his 'Treatise' (too abstruse) and subsequently recast his ideas into 'Enquiries' and 'Essays' to successfully reach the 'conversible World'.

heuristics: Consider essays as standalone works rather than looking for a strict overarching connection between them.

signature_quotes.context: Hume describing his 'ruling passion' in his autobiographical essay 'My Own Life'.

signature_quotes.text: shut up in the Colleges and Cells, and secluded from the World and Good Company
signature_quotes.context: Hume lamenting the isolation of academic learning in 'Of Essay Writing'.

signature_quotes.text: An author is little to be valued, who tells us nothing but what we can learn from every coffee-house conversation
signature_quotes.context: Hume warning against the danger of 'easy' philosophy becoming too shallow.

signature_quotes.text: not [to] look for any Connexion among these Essays, but [to] consider each of them as a Work apart
signature_quotes.context: Hume's advice to readers in the Advertisement to the 1741 edition of his Essays.

anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It is rejected by the general public, fails to influence human sentiments or conduct, and renders the work dull and irrelevant to common life.

anti_patterns.description: Writing overly 'frivolous' or 'trivial' philosophy to appease the masses.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It becomes shallow and fails to offer the reader any new insights beyond everyday 'coffee-house conversation'.
